did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a machine learning system based on a convolutional neural network (EfficientNet-B4) with transfer learning to predict the thickness of melanoma lesions from dermoscopic images, classifying them into three categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as an 'Original Article' and describes the design, implementation, and validation of a new machine learning system (using EfficientNet-B4) to predict melanoma thickness. It presents original experimental results, including a balanced accuracy of 71% on a dataset of 247 images, and compares these findings with previous studies to correct data leakage issues, rather than summarizing existing literature.

title: Design of a Machine Learning System to Predict the Thickness of a Melanoma Lesion in a Non-Invasive Way from Dermoscopic Images
brief_summary: This study aimed to develop a non-invasive machine learning system to predict the thickness of melanoma lesions from dermoscopic images to aid in identifying urgent treatment cases. The authors utilized a convolutional neural network based on the EfficientNet-B4 architecture with transfer learning, trained on the public Derm7pt dataset containing 247 images. To address class imbalance and improve generalization, the model employed image augmentation and five-fold cross-validation. The system achieved a balanced accuracy of 71% for three-way classification, outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods that suffered from data leakage. The results demonstrate that while current performance is promising, further optimization requires larger training datasets and model ensembles.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses exclusively on predicting the thickness of melanoma lesions. The dataset (Derm7pt) and experiments involve three main thickness categories: thin (<0.76 mm), intermediate (0.76-1.5 mm), and thick (>1.5 mm). Melanoma in situ was initially a separate class but was merged with the thin category for the final three-way classification task.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma lesion thickness categories (thin, intermediate, thick)
all_task_types.details: The model classifies dermoscopic images of melanoma lesions into three distinct thickness categories: thin (<0.76 mm or melanoma in situ), intermediate (0.76-1.5 mm), and thick (>1.5 mm). The paper explicitly states the task is 'three-way classification' and reports metrics such as balanced accuracy, accuracy, and F1-score for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: 7-Point Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image set containing information on lesion depth, used as the primary data source for training and validating the machine learning model.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientNet-B4 model with five-fold cross-validation. The dataset was preprocessed by cropping and resizing to 380x380 pixels. Data augmentation was applied during training.
use.validation: Used for validation via five-fold cross-validation to obtain reliable metrics. The classes 'Melanoma in situ' and 'Melanoma depth <0.76 mm' were merged into a single 'thin' class for the final three-way classification task.
use.other: The dataset was also used to reproduce and evaluate the method from Jaworek-Korjakowska et al. [11] to demonstrate the impact of data leakage. In that specific reproduction experiment, the dataset size was 244 images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the model and obtain more reliable metrics of its performance by averaging results across folds.
To create smaller subsets of the dataset with constant class ratios for performance projection and extrapolation.
To estimate performance for a more extensive dataset by minimizing mean squared error on the balanced accuracy vs. dataset size ratio.
To measure classification performance per class, averaged over classes, to address class imbalance.
To measure classification performance for each class as the average true negative rate.
To measure performance as the harmonic mean of multiclass sensitivity (ATPR) and multiclass precision.
To evaluate model performance in a 'one-versus-one' sense for all three class-pairs, averaged.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using metrics such as balanced accuracy, accuracy, ATPR, ATNR, and F1-score, with comparisons made based on metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

Derm7pt dataset (Three-way classificatio.notes: The best balanced accuracy (0.708) was achieved by training on four classes and validating on three. The reproduced method of Jaworek-Korjakowska et al. without data leakage showed significantly lower performance than their original reported scores.

Derm7pt dataset (Comparison of Backbone.metric: Accuracy, Balanced Accuracy, and ROC AUC
Derm7pt dataset (Comparison of Backbone.notes: All models were trained on four classes and validated on three classes. ROC AUC values were calculated in a 'one-versus-one' sense and averaged.

Derm7pt dataset (Reproduction of Jaworek.notes: The reproduction with leakage yielded results similar to the original paper. The reproduction without leakage showed significantly lower performance, particularly in ATPR and F1-score, indicating data leakage in the original study.

architecture_details.description: A modern convolutional neural network architecture. The authors replaced the VGG-19 architecture with EfficientNet-B4 as the backbone. EfficientNet-based models are noted for achieving exceptional accuracy on ImageNet while being smaller and faster than many other architectures.

training_details.loss_function: Weighted cross-entropy (implied by 'weighted training' and class imbalance handling, though not explicitly named as a function string)
data_augmentation: Random scaling (probability 0.85, scale factor between 0.9 and 1.1)
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Weighted training (using class weights) and merging 'Melanoma in situ' with 'Melanoma depth <0.76 mm' for the final three-way classification task

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer (implied by standard CNN architecture for classification)
class_labels: Thin (<0.76 mm or melanoma in situ)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: LIPU (logistic regression using initial variables and product units)
KDLOR (kernel discriminant learning for ordinal regression)
Jaworek-Korjakowska et al. model (CNN with VGG-19 backbone)
preprocessing_techniques: Cropping around the lesion to minimize the impact of artifacts such as oil blobs, hair, and measuring marks
Resizing images to a 1:1 aspect ratio
Resizing images to a uniform 380x380 pixel size for training and validation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rows and columns of the image were swapped with a probability of 0.5.
Each was applied independently with a probability of 0.5.
Both were carried out independently with a 20% limit in the positive and negative direction and applied with a probability of 0.75.
Applied with a probability of 0.7 using either motion blur, median blur, Gaussian blur, or Gaussian noise.
Applied with a probability of 0.7 using optical distortion, grid distortion, or elastic distortion.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Random HSV (hue, saturation, and value) shift
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Each with a probability of 0.5; with a hue shift limit of 10, a saturation shift limit of 20, and a value shift limit of 10 in both the positive and negative direction.
Applied with a probability of 0.85 and a shift limit of 0.1 (in units of image length).
With a probability of 0.85, rescaled with a random scale factor between 0.9 and 1.1.
With a probability of 0.85, the image was rotated by a random angle (±15°).

conclusions: The proposed machine learning system using EfficientNet-B4 achieved a balanced accuracy of 71% for three-way classification of melanoma lesion thickness, representing a new state-of-the-art performance on the Derm7pt dataset.

The study demonstrated that previous claims of higher performance (specifically by Jaworek-Korjakowska et al.) were erroneous due to data leakage during the evaluation process, as their reproduced results without leakage showed significantly lower performance.
Training the model on four classes and validating on three merged classes yielded the highest balanced accuracy (0.708) compared to training and validating on three classes directly.
Performance projections indicate that expanding the training dataset size and utilizing model ensembles can further optimize the system's performance.
The model's performance is currently limited by the small size of the available training dataset (247 images), which restricts its immediate real-world applicability without further data collection.
important_remarks: The study identifies and corrects a critical data leakage issue in previous state-of-the-art work (Jaworek-Korjakowska et al.), demonstrating that their reported high accuracy (87.2%) was artificially inflated because SMOTE oversampling was applied before data splitting, causing validation data to influence training.

The model's performance is limited by the small size of the training dataset (247 images from the Derm7pt dataset), which restricts immediate real-world clinical applicability and necessitates larger datasets or synthetic data generation (e.g., GANs) for optimization.

Training the model on four original classes and merging them into three classes only during the validation phase yielded a higher balanced accuracy (0.708) than training directly on the merged three classes, highlighting the benefit of preserving finer class resolution during training to mitigate bias.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons are based on metric values (balanced accuracy, F1-score) rather than statistical significance.

The system was validated exclusively on a single public dataset (Derm7pt) using five-fold cross-validation, lacking external validation on independent, multi-center clinical datasets, which limits the generalizability of the findings.
The study focuses solely on melanoma thickness prediction and does not address the classification of non-melanoma lesions, limiting its scope as a general dermatological diagnostic tool.

The authors note that while the balanced accuracy of 71% represents a new state-of-the-art on this specific dataset, it may not be adequate for standalone real-world clinical use without further optimization or ensemble methods.
